Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## TRUTHFUL SELF-PLAY

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We present a general framework for evolutionary learning to emergent unbiased state representation without any supervision. Evolutionary frameworks such as self-play converge to bad local optima in case of multi-agent reinforcement learning in non-cooperative partially observable environments with communication due to information asymmetry. Our proposed framework is a simple modification of selfplay inspired by mechanism design, also known as _reverse game theory_ , to elicit truthful signals and make the agents cooperative. The key idea is to add imaginary rewards using the peer prediction method, i.e., a mechanism for evaluating the validity of information exchanged between agents in a decentralized environment. Numerical experiments with predator prey, traffic junction and StarCraft tasks demonstrate that the state-of-the-art performance of our framework.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Evolving culture prevents deep neural networks from falling into bad local optima (Bengio, 2012). For example, self-play (Samuel, 1967; Tesauro, 1995) has not only demonstrated the ability to abstract high-dimensional state spaces as typified by AlphaGo (Silver et al., 2017), but also improved exploration coverage in partially observable environments and communication (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016; Singh et al., 2019) to exchange their internal representations, such as explored observation and hidden state in RNNs. Evolutionary learning is expected to be a general framework for creating superhuman AIs as such learning can generate a high-level abstract representation without any bias in supervision.

However, when applying evolutionary learning to a partially observable environment with noncooperative agents, improper bias is injected into the state representation. This bias originates from the environment. A partially observable environment with non-cooperative agents induces actions that disable an agent from honestly sharing the correct internal state resulting in the agent taking actions such as concealing information and deceiving other agents at equilibrium (Singh et al., 2019). The problem arises because the agent cannot fully observe the state of the environment, and thus, it does not have sufficient knowledge to verify the information provided by other agents. Furthermore, neural networks are vulnerable to adversarial examples (Szegedy et al., 2014) and are likely to induce erroneous behavior with small perturbations. Many discriminative models for information accuracy are available; these include GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014; Radford et al., 2016) and curriculum learning (Lowe et al., 2020). However, these models assume that accurate samples can be obtained by supervision. Because of this assumption, is it impossible to apply these models to a partially observable environment, where the distribution is not stable.

We generalize self-play to non-cooperative partially observable environments via mechanism design (Myerson, 1983; Miller et al., 2005), which is also known as _reverse game theory_ . The key idea is to add imaginary rewards by using the peer prediction method (Miller et al., 2005), that is, a mechanism for evaluating the validity of information exchanged between agents in a decentralized environment, which is calculated based on social influence on the signals. We formulate the non-cooperative partially observable environment as an extention of the _pertially observable stochastic games_ (POSG) (Hansen et al., 2004); introduce truthfulness (Vickrey, 1961), which is an indicator of the validity of state representation. We show that the imaginary reward enables us to reflect the bias of state representation on the gradient without oracles.

As the first contribution, we propose _truthful self-play_ (TSP) and analytically demonstrate convergence to the global optimum (Section 4). We propose the imaginary reward on the basis of the peer prediction

method (Miller et al., 2005) and apply it to self-play. The mechanism affects the gradient of the local optima, but not the global optima. The trick is to use _the actions taken by the agents_ as feedback to verify the received signal from the every other agent, instead of the true state, input, and intent, which the agents cannot fully observe. TSP only requires a modification of the baseline function for self-play; it drastically improves the convergence to the global optimum in Comm-POSG.

As the second contribution, based on the results of numerical experiments, we report that the TSP achieved state-of-the-art performance for various multi-agent tasks made of up to 20 agents (Section 5). Using predator prey (Barrett et al., 2011), traffic junction (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016; Singh et al., 2019), and StarCraft Synnaeve et al. (2016) environments, which are typically used in Comm-POSG research, we compared the performances of TSP with the current neural nets, including the state-ofthe-art method, with LSTM, CommNet (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016), and IC3Net (Singh et al., 2019). We report that the model with IC3Net optimized by TSP has the best performance.

This work is the first attempt to apply mechanism design to evolutionary learning. TSP is a general optimization algorithm whose convergence is theoretically guaranteed for arbitrary policies and environments. Furthermore, since no supervision is required, TSP has a wide range of applications to not only game AIs (Silver et al., 2017), but also the robots (Jaderberg et al., 2018), chatbots (Gupta et al., 2019; Chevalier et al., 2019), and autonomous cars (Tang, 2019) that are employed in multiagent tasks.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Neural communication has gained attention in the field of _multiagent reinforcement learning_ (MARL) for both discrete (Foerster et al., 2016) and continuous (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016; Singh et al., 2019) signals. Those networks are trained via self-play to exchange the internal state of the environment stored in the working memory of recurrent neural networks (RNNs) to learn the right policy in partially observable environments.

The term self-play was coined by the game AI community in the latter half of the century. Samuel (Samuel, 1967) introduced self-play as a framework for sharing a state-action value among two opposing agents to efficiently search the state space at Checkers. TD-Gammon (Tesauro, 1995) introduced self-play as a framework to learn TD( _λ_ ) (Sutton & Barto, 1998) and achieve professionalgrade levels in backgammon. AlphaGo (Silver et al., 2017) defeated the Go champion by combining supervised learning with professional game records and self-play. AlphaZero (Silver et al., 2018) successfully learnt beyond its own performance entirely based on self-play. All these studies explain that eliminating the bias of human knowledge in supervision is the advantage of self-play.

Self-play is also known as _evolutionary learning_ (Bengio, 2012) in the deep learning community mainly as an approach to emerging representations without supervision (Bansal et al., 2018; Balduzzi et al., 2019). Bansal et al. (2018) show that competitive environments contribute to emerging diversity and complexity. Rich generative models such as GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014; Radford et al., 2016) are frameworks for acquiring an environmental model by employing competitive settings. RNNs such as world models (Ha & Schmidhuber, 2018; Eslami et al., 2018) are capable of more comprehensive ranges of exploration in partially observable environments and generation of symbols and languages (Bengio, 2017; Gupta et al., 2019; Chevalier et al., 2019). The difference between evolutionary learning and supervised learning is the absence of human knowledge and oracles.

Several works have formalized those in which the agents exchange environmental information as a formal class of the games such as Dec-POMDP-Com (Goldman & Zilberstein, 2003) and COMMTDP (Pynadath & Tambe, 2002), and several frameworks are proposed to aim to solve the problems. However, the limitation of the frameworks is that they assume a common reward. As there are yet no formal definition of _non-cooperative_ communication game, we formalize such a game to

Comm-POSG as a superset of POSGs (Hansen et al., 2004), a more general class of multi-agent games including the cases of non-cooperativity (Hansen et al., 2004).

To the best of our knowledge, there are no studies that have introduced truthful mechanisms into the field of MARL, but it may be possible to introduce it by using agents that can learn flexibly, such as neural networks. A typical truthful mechanism is the VCG mechanism (Vickrey, 1961), which is a generalization of the pivot method used in auction theory, but whereas the subject of the report that must satisfy truthfulness must be a _valuation_ (or a value function if interpreted from a RL perspective). In this study, the scope of application is different because the belief states of the environment are subject to reporting. Therefore, we introduce instead a peer prediction method (Miller et al., 2005) that guarantees truthfulness with respect to reporting beliefs about arbitrary probability distributions using _proper scoring rules_ (Gneiting & Raftery, 2007).

## 3 PROBLEM DEFINITION

## 3.1 COMM-POSG

A _communicative partially-observable stochastic game_ (Comm-POSG) is a class of non-cooperative Bayesian games in which every agent does not fully observe the environment but interacts each other. We define Comm-POSG as an extension of POSG (Hansen et al., 2004) with a message protocol.
**Definition 3.1** (Hansen et al., 2004) POSG _⟨n, T, S, A, X , T , P, R⟩_ is a class for multi-agent decision making under uncertainty in which the state evolves over time 1 _≤ t ≤ T_ , where

- _n_ is the number of agents,

- _T_ is a horizon _i.e.,_ the episode length,

- _S_ is a set of discrete/continuous state _st ∈S_ with an initial probabilistic distribution _p_ ( _s_ 0),

- _A_ is a set of discrete/continuous action _ati ∈A_ ,

- _X_ is a set of discrete/continuous observation _xti ∈X_ ,

- _T ∈_ P ( _S × A × S_ ) is state transition probability,

- _P ∈_ P ( _S × X[n]_ ) is an observation probability, and

- _R_ : _S × A[n] →_ R _[n]_ is a reward function that outputs an _n_ -dimensional vector. □

In Comm-POSGs, every agent further follows a _message protocol Z[n][×][n]_ , where _Z_ is the discrete/continuous signal space. The complete information exchanged among the agent in time is **Z** _t_ , where **Z** _t_ := ( _ztij_ ) _i,j∈_ � _n_ � _∈Z[n][×][n]_ is a _signal matrix_ in which ( _i, j_ )-th entry _ztij_ represents a signal from Agent _i_ to Agent _j_ at _t_ . The _i_ -th diagonal entry of **Z** _t_ , _hti_ := _ztii_ represents the _pre-state_ , an internal state of _i_ -th agent before receiving the singals from the others. A game in Comm-POSG is denoted as _G_ := _⟨n, T, S, A, X , T , P, R, Z⟩_ .

The objective function of Comm-POSG is _social welfare_ (Arrow, 1963) defined by the following,

where _γ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] is discount rate, _rti_ is reward _πi_ is a stochastic policy, and _V[π][i]_ is the value function.

In extensive-form games including Comm-POSG, in addition to the information in the environment, the information of other agents cannot be observed. In the optimization problem under these assumptions, a policy converges to a solution called the Bayesian Nash equilibrium (BNE) (Fudenberg, 1993). We denote the social welfare at the BNE is _J[∗]_ , and the global maximum _J_[ˆ] . In general, _J[∗]_ = _J_[ˆ] holds, which is closely related to the information asymmetry.

Hence, the veracity of the signals **z** _ti_ is the point of contention.

## 3.3 TRUTHFULNESS

In mechanism design, a _truthful_ game (Vickrey, 1961) is a game in which all agents make an honest reporting in the Bayesian Nash equilibrium. In Comm-POSGs, the truthfulness of the game is achieved if all the sent signal equals the pre-state _ztij_ = _hti i.e.,_ all the agent share a complete information. In such case, all the agents can have the same information **ˆz** _ti_ = **h** _t_ := ( _ht_ 1 _, . . . , htn_ )[T] for all _i_ ’s. In such a case, all agents will have the same post-state model probability distribution and the mean of the cross entropy between the probability distributions of each model will be minimized.

The first term represents the entropy of knowledge each agent has about the environment, and the second term represents the information asymmetry between agents; _Dφ_ is a lower bound on the amount of true information the environment has, H [ _p_ ( _st_ )]; since achieving truthfulness is essentially the same problem as minimizing _Dφ_ , eventually it also maximizes _J[∗]_ will be maximized. **Proposition 3.1.** _(global optimally) For any games G in Comm-POSG, if Dφ_ ( **Z** _t_ ) = H [ _p_ ( _st_ )] _for_ 0 _≤ t ≤ T and Ri is symmetric for any permutation of i ∈_ � _n_ � _, J[∗]_ ( _G_ ) = _J_[ˆ] ( _G_ ) _holds_[1] _._

## 4 PROPOSED FRAMEWORK

An obvious way to achieve truthful learning is to add _Dφ_ as a penalty term of the objective, but there are two obstacles to this approach. One is that the new regularization term also adds a bias to the social welfare _J_ , and the other is that _Dφ_ contains the agent’s internal state, post-state _h_[ˆ] _ti_ , so the exact amount cannot be measured by the designer of the learner. If post-states are reported correctly, then pre-states should also be reported honestly, and thus truthfulness is achieved. Therefore, it must be assumed that the post-states cannot be observed during optimization.

Our framework, _truthful self-play_ (TSP), consists of two elements: one is the introduction of _imaginary rewards_ , a general framework for unbiased regularization in Comm-POSG, and the other is the introduction of peer prediction method (Miller et al., 2005), a truthful mechanism to encourage honest reporting based solely on observable variables. In the following, each of them is described separately and we clarify that the proposed framework converges to the global optimum in Comm-POSG. We show the whole procedure in Algorithm 1.

## 4.2 PEER PREDICTION MECHANISM

The _peer prediction mechanism_ (Miller et al., 2005) is derived from a mechanism design using _proper scoring rules_ (Gneiting & Raftery, 2007), which aims to encourage verifiers to honestly report their beliefs by assigning a reward measure score to their responses when predicting probabilistic events. These mechanisms assume at least two agents, a reporter and a verifier.

The general scoring rule can be expressed as _F_ ( _ps∥s_ ) where _ps_ is the probability of occurrence reported by the verifier for the event _s_ , and _F_ ( _ps∥s_ ) is the score to be obtained if the reported event _s_ actually occurred. The scoring rule is _proper_ if an honest declaration consistent with the beliefs of the verifier and the reported _ps_ maximizes the expected value of the earned score, and it is _strictly proper_ if it is the only option for maximizing the expected value. A representative example that is strictly proper is the logarithmic scoring rule _F_ ( _ps∥s_ ) = log _ps_ , where the expected value for a 1-bit signal is the cross-entropy _p[∗] s_[log] _[ p][s]_[+ (1] _[ −][p][∗] s_[) log(1] _[ −][p][s]_[)][ for belief] _[ p][∗] s_[. One can find that] _[ p][s]_[=] _[ p][∗] s_[is the] only report that maximizes the score.

Since the proper scoring rule assumes that events _s_ are observable, it is not applicable to problems such as partial observation environments where the true value is hidden. Miller et al. (2005), who first presented a peer prediction mechanism, posed scoring to a posterior of the verifiers that are updated by the signal, rather than the event. This concept is formulated by a model that assumes that an event _s_ emits a signal _z_ stochastically and infers the type of _s_ by the signal of the reporters who receive it. The peer prediction mechanism (Miller et al., 2005) is denoted as _F_ ( _p_ ( _s|z_ ) _∥s_ ) under the assumption that ( **1** ) the type of event _s_ and the signal _z_ emitted by each type follow a given prior, ( **2** ) the priors are shared knowledge among verifiers, and ( **3** ) the posterior is updated according to the reports.

We apply the mechanism to RL, _i.e.,_ the problem of predicting the agent’s optimal behavior _ati ∼ πθ|st_ for the true state _st ∈S_ . In self-play, the conditions of **1** and **2** can be satisfied because the prior _πθ_ is shared among the agents, and furthermore, the post-state in Comm-POSG corresponds to **3** , so that the peer prediction mechanism can be applied to the problem of predicting agent behavior. To summarize the above discussion, we can see that we can allocate a _score matrix Lt_ as follows,

## 4.3 THE TRUTHFUL SELF-PLAY

In our framework, a truthful mechanism is constructed by introducing the proper prediction mechanism into imaginary rewards. Since the score matrix _Lt_ does not satisfy Hermitianity, we perform zero-averaging by subtracting the mean of the scores from each element of the matrix, thereby making the sum to zero. ## 5 NUMERICAL EXPERIMENT

In this section, we establish the convergence of TSP through the results of numerical experiments with deep neural nets. We consider three environments for our analysis and experiments ( _→_ Fig. 1). ( **a** ) a predator prey environment (PP) in which predators with limited vision look for a prey on a square grid. ( **b** ) a traffic junction environment (TJ) similar to Sukhbaatar et al. (2016) in which agents with limited vision learn to signal in order to avoid collisions. ( **c** ) StarCraft: Brood Wars (SC) explore and combat tasks which test control on multiple agents in various scenarios where agent needs to understand and decouple observations for multiple opposing units.

We compare the performances of TSP with self-play (SP) and SP with curiosity (Houthooft et al., 2016) using three tasks belonging to Comm-POSG, comprising up to 20 agents. The hyperparameters are listed in the appendix. With regard CRAs, three models namely LSTM, CommNet (Sukhbaatar et al., 2016), and IC3Net (Singh et al., 2019), were compared. The empirical mean of the social welfare function _J_ was used as a measure for the comparison. IC3Net is an improvement over CommNet, which is a continuous communication method based on LSTM. Actor-critic and value functions were added to the baselines in all the frameworks. We performed 2,000 epochs of experiment with 500 steps, each using 120 CPUs; the experiment was conducted over a period of three days.

**PP and TJ:** Table 1 lists the experimental results for each task. We can see that IC3Net with TSP outperforms the one with SP for all tasks. Fig. 2 (a) shows that TSP elicits truthful information, and (b) confirms that the social welfare of TSP exceeds that of the SPs. (c) confirms that the average of the imaginary part is zero. From these experimental results, we conclude that the TSP successfully realized truthful learning and state-of-the-art in tasks comprising 3 to 20 agents.

## 6 CONCLUDING REMARK

Our objective was to construct a general framework for emergent unbiased state representation without any supervision. Firstly, we proposed the TSP and theoretically clarified its convergence to the global optimum in the general case. Secondly, we performed experiments involving up to 20 agents and achieved the state-of-the-art performance for all the tasks. Herein, we summarize the advantages of our framework.
1. **Strong convergence** : TSP guarantees convergence to the global optimum theoretically and experimentally; self-play cannot provide such a guarantee. Besides, the imaginary reward _**i**_ ∆ _L_

2. **Simple solution** : The only modification required for TSP is that _**i**_ ∆ _L_ should be added to the baseline in order to easily implement it for deep learning software libraries such as TensorFlow and PyTorch.

3. **Broad coverage** : TSP is a general framework, the same as self-play. Since TSP is independent of both agents and environments and supports both discrete and continuous control, it can be applied to a wide range of domains. No supervision is required.

To the best of our knowledge, introducing mechanism design to MARL is a new direction for the deep-learning community. In future work, we will consider fairness (Sen, 1984) as the social choice function. We expect that many other frameworks will be developed by using the methodology employed in this study.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes an ambitious and conceptually novel integration of mechanism design—specifically peer prediction—into multi-agent reinforcement learning via a self-play framework. The idea of using imaginary rewards to incentivize truthful communication without supervision is creative and aligns with growing interest in aligning learned incentives with cooperative behavior. The work draws from solid foundations in game theory and scoring rules, and the experimental domains are standard and reasonably diverse.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical convergence claim lacks sufficient justification: The paper claims in Theorem 4.1 that TSP converges to the global optimum under a bounded mass parameter condition (Eq 9), but the proof is extremely terse and does not establish a clear link between the peer prediction signal, policy gradients, and convergence in a stochastic, high-dimensional RL setting. The argument relies on Proposition 4.1, which assumes the game is truthful and the reward matrix is Hermitian, but it is not shown that the peer prediction mechanism actually induces truthfulness in the CRA agents. The condition in Eq 9 involves a supremum over the ratio of real and imaginary value gradients, which is not analyzed or justified—readers are left to assume it holds without understanding its plausibility in practice. This undermines the paper’s central theoretical contribution.

- Peer prediction mechanism is misapplied to high-dimensional, continuous state spaces: The peer prediction method (Miller et al., 2005) assumes discrete signals and known priors over events, but the paper applies it to continuous, high-dimensional pre-states $h_{ti}$ communicated between RNNs. Equation 6 uses $\log \pi_\theta(a_{ti}|z_{tji})$ as a scoring rule, but this evaluates action likelihood under a message, not the consistency between agents’ beliefs about a latent state. This is not a valid peer prediction mechanism: it scores how well one agent’s message predicts another’s action, not whether the message reflects a truthful belief. Without a model of how messages relate to ground-truth state or beliefs, the mechanism cannot incentivize truthfulness as claimed. This conceptual mismatch invalidates the core mechanism.

- No behavioral validation of truthfulness: The paper claims to elicit truthful communication but provides no direct measurement of truthfulness beyond a single plot (Fig 2a) in one environment showing the fraction of time $z_{ij} = h_i$. This equality is trivially false in continuous or high-dimensional spaces, and even in discrete settings, it is not clear how exact equality is determined. There is no analysis of message fidelity, information leakage, or deception. Without quantifying how much information is truthfully shared or how the mechanism reduces information asymmetry (e.g., via mutual information or KL divergence), the central claim of 'truthful' self-play remains unsubstantiated. Performance gains could stem from regularization or exploration rather than truthfulness.

- Imaginary rewards lack theoretical grounding and practical necessity: The use of complex-valued rewards with real and imaginary parts is presented as a novel mechanism, but it appears to be a notational rebranding of a multi-objective reward shaping. The imaginary part is zero-sum (via the Laplacian), so it functions as an internal redistribution of incentives. However, the paper does not explain why this complex formalism is needed over standard reward shaping or counterfactual baselines. The claim that it 'does not affect the social welfare' is trivial for any zero-sum internal transfer, and the connection to policy gradient updates (Eq 5) is equivalent to adding a scaled auxiliary loss. The complex number framework adds opacity without clear benefit.

- Evaluation lacks key baselines and ablation: The paper compares TSP only against standard self-play and curiosity-driven baselines, but omits state-of-the-art methods that explicitly address communication and incentive alignment, such as I2C, COMA, or TarMAC. More critically, there is no ablation of the peer prediction component: we do not know whether performance gains come from the specific scoring rule, the zero-sum reward structure, or simply adding any auxiliary signal. Without ablating $\Delta \mathcal{L}_t$ or testing alternative scoring functions, the contribution of the proposed mechanism cannot be isolated. This makes it impossible to attribute success to the core idea.

- Overstatement of generality and applicability: The paper claims TSP is a 'general framework' applicable to arbitrary policies and environments, but the method depends on agents having access to each other’s messages and policies (to compute $\log \pi_\theta(a_{ti}|z_{tji})$), which requires centralized training or policy sharing. This contradicts the decentralized execution setting implied by Comm-POSG. Moreover, the approach assumes policies are differentiable and messages are interpretable as state estimates—strong assumptions that limit generality. The claim of no supervision needed is misleading, as the entire mechanism is hand-designed with strong structural assumptions about agent models and incentives.

- No verification that peer prediction induces truthfulness: The paper claims TSP elicits truthful communication via peer prediction, but provides no evidence that the mechanism actually aligns incentives for truth-telling in the tested environments. A standard validation in mechanism design is to show that truthful reporting is a dominant strategy or Bayesian Nash equilibrium under the proposed scoring rule. The paper should compute, for a simplified case (e.g., binary state, two agents), whether deviating from truthful reporting reduces expected peer reward. Without this, the core claim that agents are incentivized to be truthful remains unsupported, and performance gains could stem from other effects like regularization or increased message salience.

- Missing ablation of imaginary reward mechanism: The paper attributes performance gains to the peer prediction-based imaginary reward, but offers no ablation isolating its effect. A critical test would be to replace $\Delta \mathcal{L}_t$ with a zero-sum but non-informational reward (e.g., random scores or constant offsets) and compare performance. If gains persist, the improvement may stem from the zero-sum structure itself rather than truthfulness. Alternatively, removing the imaginary reward entirely while keeping other components would clarify its necessity. Without such ablations, the contribution of the proposed mechanism cannot be disentangled from generic multi-agent credit assignment effects.

- No analysis of convergence condition in practice: Theorem 4.1 relies on a convergence condition involving the ratio of real and imaginary value gradients, yet the paper does not evaluate whether this condition holds during training. For the theory to have bite, the authors should plot $|\partial \operatorname{Re} V / \partial \operatorname{Im} V|$ over time in one of the experiments and verify it remains below $\beta$. If this ratio spikes or exceeds the bound, the theoretical guarantee fails, and the observed convergence would need an alternative explanation. This computation is essential to show the theory connects to practice.

- Lack of comparison to communication-aware baselines: The evaluation omits key baselines that address similar challenges, such as I2C (which incentivizes informative communication) or TarMAC (which uses attention-based communication). These methods also aim to improve coordination in partially observable settings and would provide a more meaningful comparison than standard self-play. The paper should include at least one such method to demonstrate that TSP’s gains are not simply due to better communication structure but stem from the specific mechanism design component. Without this, the 'state-of-the-art' claim is inadequately supported.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a rigorous proof or formal argument that the peer prediction mechanism induces truthful reporting in the CRA setting, including assumptions on message space, policy expressiveness, and observability.

- Reformulate the peer prediction scoring rule to properly assess belief consistency, or justify why action prediction likelihood serves as a valid proxy for truthfulness.

- Include ablation studies that isolate the effect of the peer prediction signal and the imaginary reward structure on performance and truthfulness metrics.

- Add behavioral analyses of communication fidelity, such as mutual information between messages and true state, or KL divergence between agent belief states, across all environments.

- Compare against stronger baselines that address communication and incentives in MARL, such as I2C, COMA, or other mechanism design-inspired methods.

comments.title: Incorrect Hermitian condition invalidates social welfare preservation
comments.quote: rther, since  $\mathbf{R}^+$  is Hermitian,  $iy_{ij} = -iy_{ji}$ , and hence  $\operatorname {Im}\hat{J} (\mathcal{G}[i\mathbf{Y}]) = 0$  holds;  $\hat{J} (\mathcal{G}[i\mathbf{Y}]) = \hat{J} (\mathcal{G})$  holds.
comments.explanation: The claim that $\mathbf{R}^+$ being Hermitian implies $iy_{ij} = -iy_{ji}$ is algebraically incorrect. A Hermitian matrix satisfies $R_{ij}^+ = \overline{R_{ji}^+}$. For the imaginary reward $iy_{ij}$, this means $iy_{ij} = \overline{iy_{ji}} = -i y_{ji}$, which implies $y_{ij} = y_{ji}$, not antisymmetry. Thus, the conclusion that the imaginary part of total welfare vanishes due to antisymmetry is invalid. The correct condition $y_{ij} = y_{ji}$ contradicts the intended zero-sum redistribution, undermining the argument that imaginary rewards preserve social welfare while shaping incentives.

comments.title: Graph Laplacian misapplied in zero-averaging
comments.quote:

\[ \mathbf{Y}_{t}=\Delta\mathcal{L}_{
comments.explanation: The operation $\Delta \mathcal{L}_t$ with $\Delta = I - \mathbf{11}^T/n$ centers the rows of $\mathcal{L}_t$, not the entire matrix. This ensures each row has mean zero, but the total sum of $\mathbf{Y}_t$ is not necessarily zero unless all row sums are equal. The paper claims this achieves 'zero-averaging' to make the sum of imaginary rewards zero, but this is false in general. True zero-sum redistribution would require subtracting the global mean $\frac{1}{n^2}\mathbf{11}^T\mathcal{L}_t\mathbf{11}^T$, not applying the graph Laplacian. The current formulation fails to ensure conservation of imaginary welfare.

comments.title: Proof of Theorem 4.1 cites non-existent proposition
comments.quote:
Proof 4.1.

(in summary.) $\mathbf{R}+i\Delta\mathcal{L}$ is Hermitian; and if Eq 9 holds, then $\mathcal{G}[i\Delta\mathcal{L}]$ is truthful from Proposition 1. Therefore, from Proposition 4.1, convergence to the global optima is achieved. ∎
comments.explanation: The proof relies on 'Proposition 1' to establish that the bounded gradient condition (Eq 9) implies truthfulness of $\mathcal{G}[i\Delta\mathcal{L}]$, but no such proposition exists in the paper. Section 4.1 contains only Proposition 4.1, which assumes truthfulness rather than derives it. This breaks the logical chain: the paper asserts convergence to the global optimum based on a missing argument. Without a derivation linking Eq 9 to incentive alignment or truthful equilibria, the central theoretical claim lacks foundation.

comments.title: Hermitianity of $\mathbf{R}^+$ not justified
comments.quote:
our framework, a truthful mechanism is constructed by introducing the proper prediction mechanism into imaginary rewards. Since the score matrix $\mathcal{L}_{t}$ does not satisfy Hermitianity, we perform zero-averaging by subtracting the mean of the scores from each element of the matrix, thereby making the sum to zero. This can be expressed as follows using the graph Laplacian $\Delta:=E-\mathbf{11}^{\text{T}}/n$.

\[ \mathbf{Y}_{t}=\Delta\mathcal{L}_{t}=\frac{1}{n}\left[\begin{array}[]{cccc}n-1&-1&.
comments.explanation: The paper claims $\mathbf{R}_t^+ = \mathbf{R}_t + i\Delta\mathcal{L}_t$ is Hermitian, but this requires $\Delta\mathcal{L}_t$ to be skew-symmetric. While $\Delta$ is symmetric, $\mathcal{L}_t$ is generally asymmetric because $\log \pi_\theta(a_{tj}|z_{tij}) \neq \log \pi_\theta(a_{ti}|z_{tji})$. The product of a symmetric and asymmetric matrix is not guaranteed to be skew-symmetric. For example, with $n=2$ and $\mathcal{L}_t = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1 \\ 2 & 0\end{bmatrix}$, $\Delta\mathcal{L}_t$ is not skew-symmetric. Thus, $\mathbf{R}_t^+$ is not Hermitian, invalidating the use of Proposition 4.1, which depends on this property.

comments.title: Convergence condition (Eq 9) is mathematically ill-defined
comments.quote:
Theorem 4.1. (global optimally) For any games in Comm-POSG, TSP converges to the global optimum $\hat{J}$ if the following convergence condition are met,

$\sup_{\phi}\left|\frac{\partial\operatorname{Re}V^{\pi_{\theta}}}{\partial\operatorname{Im}V^{\pi_{\theta}}}\right|<\beta,$ (9)

where
comments.explanation: The expression $\partial \operatorname{Re}V / \partial \operatorname{Im}V$ is not standard in RL or optimization. The real and imaginary parts of the value function are not independent variables, so this derivative lacks a clear functional interpretation. Moreover, the supremum is over $\phi$, but $\phi$ is not the policy parameter in the value function $V^{\pi_\theta}$—$\theta$ is. This mismatch suggests a notational error. The condition is presented as universally applicable, yet its mathematical meaning is ambiguous, making it impossible to verify in theory or practice.

comments.title: Conditional independence of signals not established
comments.quote: The peer prediction mechanism *(Miller et al., 2005)* is denoted as $\mathcal{F}(p(s|z)\|s)$ under the assumption that (1) the type of event $s$ and the signal $z$ emitted by each type follow a given prior, (2) the priors are shared knowledge among verifiers, and (3) the posterior is updated according to the reports.
comments.explanation: Peer prediction mechanisms require that agents' signals are conditionally independent given the hidden state $s_t$, i.e., $p(z_i,z_j|s) = p(z_i|s)p(z_j|s)$. This ensures that agreement is most likely under truthful reporting. The paper does not establish this condition. In self-play with shared policies, agents may produce correlated messages due to common training, violating conditional independence. This allows agents to coordinate on uninformative but consistent signals, maximizing peer rewards without revealing truthful information about $s_t$.

comments.title: Truthfulness metric based on exact equality in continuous space
comments.quote: Fig. 2 (a) shows that TSP elicits truthful information, and (b) confirms that the social welfare of TSP exceeds that of the SPs. (c) confirms that the average of the imaginary part is zero. From these experimental results, we conclude that the TSP successfully realized truthful learning and state-of-the-art in tasks comprising 3 to 20 agents.
comments.explanation: Figure 2a measures truthfulness as the fraction of steps where $z_{ij} = h_i$, implying exact equality. In deep RL with continuous, high-dimensional vectors, exact equality is numerically impossible due to floating-point precision and stochasticity. The non-zero values suggest an implicit discretization or thresholding, but this is not described. A meaningful metric would use a continuous similarity measure like $\|z_{ij} - h_i\|^2$ or mutual information. The current metric lacks rigor and does not reflect the actual representation space.

comments.title: Zero average imaginary reward does not imply truthfulness
comments.quote: (c) confirms that the average of the imaginary part is zero. From these experimental results, we conclude that the TSP successfully realized truthful learning and state-of-the-art in tasks comprising 3 to 20 agents.
comments.explanation: The average of the imaginary reward converging to zero is expected by construction—it is a zero-sum signal. This occurs under any stable equilibrium, truthful or not. For example, if agents coordinate on a consistent but false message, the imaginary reward would still average to zero. Thus, this observation is necessary but not sufficient for truthfulness. The paper provides no additional behavioral validation to rule out coordinated deception.

comments.title: Misleading analogy to policy gradient baseline
comments.quote: The baseline function in policy gradient *Williams (1992)* is an example of a function that does not affect the objective function when the mean gets zero. However, the baseline function is a quantity that is determined based on the value function of a single agent, whereas the imaginary reward is different in that (1) it affects the value function of each agent and (2) it is a meaningful quantity only when $n\geq 2$ and is not observed when $n=1$.
comments.explanation: The analogy to the policy gradient baseline is misleading. A baseline reduces variance without biasing the gradient because it is action-independent. The imaginary reward $i\Delta\mathcal{L}_t$ directly modifies the gradient via $\alpha\beta \operatorname{Im} \mathbf{g}_t$, changing the update direction unless $\beta=0$. It alters agent incentives and thus the learned policy, unlike a true baseline. The paper conflates zero-sum redistribution with baseline invariance, which are distinct concepts.

comments.title: Social welfare sign convention contradicts terminology
comments.quote: Table 1: Social welfare  $J$  in five Comm-POSG tasks. PP-  $n$  : Predator prey and TJ-  $n$  : Traffic junction. All the scores are negatives. The experiment was repeated thrice. The average and standard deviation are listed. Bold is the highest score.
comments.explanation: The term 'social welfare' implies a quantity to be maximized, but Table 1 presents negative values where lower (more negative) is worse, and the best results are closest to zero. This indicates $J$ is a cost or penalty function, minimized during training. The use of 'welfare' without clarification contradicts standard terminology and risks misinterpretation. The caption should explicitly state that $J$ is a cumulative penalty to be minimized.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Truthful Self-Play (TSP), a framework combining self-play with peer prediction mechanisms to address information asymmetry in non-cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning. While the idea of using mechanism design to incentivize truthful communication is novel, the paper suffers from several critical issues: the theoretical convergence proof appears incomplete and relies on questionable assumptions, the experimental results contradict the claimed performance improvements, and the peer prediction implementation deviates from standard formulations without justification.

The paper presents an interesting intersection of mechanism design and multi-agent reinforcement learning, attempting to solve the important problem of biased state representations in non-cooperative environments. The core idea of using imaginary rewards based on peer prediction is creative and could be valuable if properly developed. However, the execution falls short on multiple fronts - the theoretical foundations are shaky, the experimental validation is weak, and key methodological choices are inadequately justified.

Overview issues:

- Incomplete convergence proof: Theorem 4.1 claims global optimality but the proof is insufficient. The condition $\
\sup_{\phi}|\partial \operatorname{Re}V^{\pi_{\theta}}/\partial \operatorname{Im}V^{\pi_{\theta}}|<\beta$ appears without derivation or justification. The proof merely states that if Eq 9 holds, then the game becomes truthful from Proposition 1, but Proposition 1 only establishes that truthfulness implies $J^* = \hat{J}$ under symmetric rewards - it doesn't show how the peer prediction mechanism ensures truthfulness. The critical link between the scoring rule and achieving truthful equilibrium is missing.

- Contradictory experimental results: Table 1 shows TSP achieving worse performance (higher negative scores) than standard self-play across all tasks. For PP-3, SP-LSTM achieves 1.92 while TSP-IC3Net gets 0.69. Similarly in TJ-20, SP-LSTM gets 819.97 vs TSP's 132.60. The authors claim TSP "achieved state-of-the-art performance" but the data clearly shows otherwise. Figure 2b shows TSP's real reward component is lower than SP's, yet the conclusion ignores this. The StarCraft results in Table 2 also show TSP underperforming baselines. This fundamentally undermines the paper's main claim.

- Peer prediction implementation deviates from standard formulation: Equation 3 defines the peer prediction score as $\ell_{tij} = \log \pi_{\theta}(a_{ti}|z_{tji})$, which uses the action policy to evaluate messages. Standard peer prediction mechanisms (Miller et al. 2005) evaluate reported signals against posterior beliefs, not actions. The paper provides no justification for this deviation. The zero-averaging using the graph Laplacian in Equation 7 is also non-standard and lacks theoretical grounding in the peer prediction literature.

- Unclear relationship between truthfulness and social welfare: Proposition 3.1 claims that if $D_{\phi}(\mathbf{Z}_{t}) = \mathbb{H}[p(s_{t})]$ then $J^* = \hat{J}$ under symmetric rewards. However, the paper doesn't establish why minimizing information asymmetry necessarily maximizes social welfare in non-cooperative games. In competitive settings, information asymmetry can be strategically valuable. The assumption that symmetric rewards are required for global optimality severely limits the framework's applicability to truly non-cooperative scenarios.

- Missing analysis of collusion and equilibrium strategies: The paper acknowledges potential collusion but provides no analysis. In peer prediction, agents can collude by reporting correlated signals to manipulate scores. The proposed framework uses agents' actions as verification, but nothing prevents agents from coordinating on both actions and messages. The literature context flags collusion resistance as critical, yet the paper offers no theoretical or empirical examination of how TSP prevents such manipulation.

- No demonstration of non-vacuous truthfulness condition: The paper's theoretical framework hinges on achieving truthfulness, but it never shows this condition is attainable in practice. For a peer prediction mechanism to be meaningful, there must exist at least one concrete game structure where agents converge to truthful reporting. The authors should compute the equilibrium for a simple 2-agent Comm-POSG (e.g., a binary signaling game) and verify that the peer prediction scores incentivize $z_{ij}=h_i$ at equilibrium. Without this worked example, readers cannot assess whether the truthfulness condition is ever satisfied outside of trivial cases.

- Missing analysis of convergence dynamics: The paper claims TSP converges to global optimum but provides no analysis of the learning dynamics. Readers need to see how the imaginary reward component affects the gradient flow compared to standard self-play. A simple simulation showing policy updates with and without $i\Delta\mathcal{L}$ would help. For instance, track the gradient components in a 3-agent predator-prey scenario and demonstrate that the imaginary term reduces divergence between agents' post-state distributions. This is essential because the mass parameter $\beta$ appears in the convergence condition but its effect is never illustrated.

- No verification of baseline condition claim: The paper states that the imaginary reward $i\Delta\mathcal{L}$ satisfies the baseline condition, but this is never proven or demonstrated. For policy gradient methods, a baseline must have zero mean to not affect the expected gradient. The authors should show that $\mathbb{E}[\Delta\mathcal{L}_t] = 0$ holds for their scoring rule. This requires computing the expectation over the joint distribution of actions and messages, or at least verifying it empirically in one of their test environments. Without this verification, the claim that TSP only modifies the baseline without affecting the objective remains unsubstantiated.

- Unexplored implementation guidance for high-dimensional settings: The paper claims TSP is a general framework applicable to complex domains but provides no guidance on practical implementation challenges. The peer prediction mechanism requires computing a full $n \times n$ score matrix, which becomes costly for large $n$. The authors should discuss computational complexity and potential approximations. For the StarCraft experiments with 20 agents, how was the score matrix efficiently computed? Is there a threshold where the method becomes infeasible? This implementation discussion is necessary for the claimed broad applicability.

Recommendation: reject

Revision targets:

- Provide a complete proof of Theorem 4.1 with rigorous derivation of the convergence condition

- Reconcile the contradictory experimental results or provide new experiments supporting the claims

- Justify the non-standard peer prediction implementation and analyze its truthfulness properties

- Clarify the theoretical relationship between truthfulness and social welfare in non-cooperative settings

- Include analysis of collusion resistance and equilibrium manipulation strategies

comments.title: Experimental results contradict state-of-the-art claims
comments.quote: As the second contribution, based on the results of numerical experiments, we report that the TSP achieved state-of-the-art performance for various multi-agent tasks made of up to 20 agents (Section 5). Using predator prey *(Barrett et al., 2011)*, traffic junction *(Sukhbaatar et al., 2016; Singh et al., 2019)*, and StarCraft *Synnaeve et al. (2016)* environments, which are typically used in Comm-POSG research, we compared the performances of TSP with the current neural nets, including the state-of-the-art method, with LSTM, CommNet *(Sukhbaatar et al., 2016)*, and IC3Net *(Singh et al., 2019)*. We report that the model with IC3Net optimized by TSP has the best performance.
comments.explanation: This claim directly contradicts the experimental results presented in the paper. According to Table 1, TSP-IC3Net achieves worse performance than SP-LSTM across all tasks: in PP-3, SP-LSTM gets 1.92 while TSP-IC3Net gets 0.69; in TJ-20, SP-LSTM gets 819.97 vs TSP's 132.60. Similarly, Table 2 shows TSP underperforming baselines in StarCraft experiments. The claim that TSP "achieved state-of-the-art performance" and "has the best performance" is factually incorrect based on the paper's own data. Rewrite this passage to accurately reflect the experimental findings.

comments.title: StarCraft table caption contradicts results
comments.quote: Table 2: StarCraft Results: TSP beats the baselines. The experiment was repeated thrice. All the scores are negatives. The average and standard deviation are listed. Bold is the highest score.
comments.explanation: The table caption claims "TSP beats the baselines" but the data shows the opposite. For Explore-10Medic, IC3Net achieves 2.276 while TSP-IC3Net gets 1.364. For Combat-10Mv3Ze, IC3Net gets 3.815 vs TSP's 1.717. Since higher (less negative) scores are better, TSP performs worse than both baselines. The caption should be corrected to reflect that TSP underperforms the baselines in StarCraft tasks.

comments.title: Convergence proof references non-existent proposition
comments.quote: (in summary.) $\mathbf{R}+i\Delta\mathcal{L}$ is Hermitian; and if Eq 9 holds, then $\mathcal{G}[i\Delta\mathcal{L}]$ is truthful from Proposition 1. Therefore, from Proposition 4.1, convergence to the global optima is achieved. ∎
comments.explanation: The proof references 'Proposition 1' but this proposition is not defined in the paper. The cross-reference section shows Proposition 3.1 and Proposition 4.1, but no Proposition 1 exists. This creates a logical gap in the proof chain. The proof claims truthfulness follows from Proposition 1, but without defining this proposition, readers cannot verify this critical step. Remove the reference to 'Proposition 1' and replace it with the actual proposition that establishes truthfulness.

comments.title: Unjustified convergence condition
comments.quote:
Theorem 4.1. (global optimally) For any games in Comm-POSG, TSP converges to the global optimum $\hat{J}$ if the following convergence condition are met,

$\sup_{\phi}\left|\frac{\partial\operatorname{Re}V^{\pi_{\theta}}}{\partial\operatorname{Im}V^{\pi_{\theta}}}\right|<\beta,$ (9)

where $\beta<\infty$ is bounded mass parameter.
comments.explanation: The convergence condition in Equation (9) appears without any derivation or justification. The paper does not explain why this specific condition involving the supremum of the ratio of partial derivatives ensures convergence, nor does it connect this condition to the peer prediction mechanism or truthfulness. The condition seems arbitrary and lacks theoretical grounding. Add a derivation showing how this condition emerges from the analysis of the learning dynamics.

comments.title: Peer prediction mechanism deviates from standard formulation
comments.quote: We apply the mechanism to RL, $i.e.$, the problem of predicting the agent’s optimal behavior $a_{ti}\sim\pi_{\theta}|s_{t}$ for the true state $s_{t}\in\mathcal{S}$. In self-play, the conditions of 1 and 2 can be satisfied because the prior $\pi_{\theta}$ is shared among the agents, and furthermore, the post-state in Comm-POSG corresponds to 3, so that the peer prediction mechanism can be applied to the problem of predicting agent behavior.
comments.explanation: The paper applies peer prediction to score actions directly using $\ell(a_{ti}|z_{tji}) = \log\pi_{\theta}(a_{ti}|z_{tji})$, which deviates from the standard peer prediction formulation $\mathcal{F}(p(s|z)\|s)$. Earlier the paper correctly states that peer prediction scores posterior beliefs about events given signals, but Equation 6 scores actions using the policy directly. The paper provides no justification for this fundamental deviation from Miller et al. (2005)'s formulation.

comments.title: Incorrect expectation notation in multi-agent setting
comments.quote: $J:=\sum_{i=1}^{n}V^{\pi_{i}};\qquad V^{\pi_{i}}:=\mathbb{E}_{\pi_{i}}\left[\sum_{t=1}^{T}\gamma^{t-1}r_{ti}\right],$ (1)
comments.explanation: The expectation notation is incorrect for multi-agent settings. In a POSG, agent $i$'s value depends on all agents' policies due to strategic interactions. The expectation $\mathbb{E}_{\pi_{i}}$ incorrectly suggests only agent $i$'s policy matters. The correct formulation should be $\mathbb{E}_{\pi_{1},...,\pi_{n}}$ or $\mathbb{E}_{\boldsymbol{\pi}}$ where $\boldsymbol{\pi} = (\pi_{1},...,\pi_{n})$. This matters because BNE analysis requires considering how each agent's policy affects others' payoffs.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in message vector definition
comments.quote: $q_{\phi}(\hat{h}_{ti}|\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{ti})$ is a stochastic model for a post-state $\hat{h}_{ti}\in\mathcal{Z}$ and the received messages $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{ti}:=\mathbf{Z}_{t:i}^{\mathsf{T}}=(z_{t1i},\ldots,z_{t,i-1,i},h_{ti},z_{t,i+1,i},\ldots,z_{tni})^{\mathsf{T}}$
comments.explanation: The definition creates a logical inconsistency: $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{ti}$ is defined as the transpose of column $i$ of matrix $\mathbf{Z}_t$, but then explicitly includes $h_{ti}$ as an element. Since $\mathbf{Z}_{t:ij} = z_{tij}$ represents messages between agents, $h_{ti}$ (a pre-state) cannot simultaneously be an element of $\mathbf{Z}_t$ and a separate quantity. Rewrite $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{ti}:=(z_{t1i},\ldots,z_{t,i-1,i},z_{t,i+1,i},\ldots,z_{tni})^{\mathsf{T}}$ and clarify that $q_{\phi}$ takes both $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{ti}$ and $h_{ti}$ as inputs.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated lower bound claim
comments.quote: $D_{\phi}$ is a lower bound on the amount of true information the environment has, $\mathbb{H}\left[p(s_{t})\right]$
comments.explanation: The claim that $D_{\phi} \leq \mathbb{H}[p(s_{t})]$ is not justified. Consider a simple counterexample: let $p(s_t)$ be a Bernoulli(0.5) distribution with entropy $\mathbb{H}[p(s_t)] = \ln 2$. Suppose each agent has a posterior $q_\phi(\hat{h}_t|\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{ti})$ that is Bernoulli(0.9) with entropy $\approx 0.325$. Then $\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \mathbb{H}[q_\phi] \approx 0.325 < \ln 2$, but if agents have different posteriors, the KL divergence terms add positive values, potentially making $D_{\phi} > \mathbb{H}[p(s_t)]$. Remove this claim or provide a mathematical derivation.

comments.title: Missing justification for BNE improvement claim
comments.quote: This indicates that $J^{*}(\mathcal{G}[\mathbf{iY}])\geq J^{*}(\mathcal{G})$, indicating that the BNE could be improved by introducing imaginary rewards.
comments.explanation: The paper claims $J^{*}(\mathcal{G}[\mathbf{iY}])\geq J^{*}(\mathcal{G})$ but provides no justification for this inequality. Proposition 4.1 only establishes equality $J^{*}(\mathcal{G}[i\mathbf{Y}]) = \hat{J}(\mathcal{G})$ under the assumption that $\mathcal{G}[i\mathbf{Y}]$ is truthful. The paper never shows that $\hat{J}(\mathcal{G}) \geq J^{*}(\mathcal{G})$ or that introducing imaginary rewards necessarily improves the BNE. Add a lemma or proof showing that for any Comm-POSG game, the global optimum $\hat{J}(\mathcal{G})$ is an upper bound on the BNE social welfare $J^{*}(\mathcal{G})$.

comments.title: Matrix index notation is mathematically invalid
comments.quote: $\mathcal{L}_{t}:=(\ell(a_{ti}|z_{tji}))_{i,j\in\frac{3}{4}n_{x}^{6}};\hskip 14.22636pt\ell(a_{ti}|z_{tji}):=\mathcal{F}(\pi_{\theta}(a_{ti}|z_{tji})||a_{ti})=\log\pi_{\theta}(a_{ti}|z_{tji}),$ (6)
comments.explanation: The matrix index notation $(i,j\in\frac{3}{4}n_{x}^{6})$ is mathematically invalid. The expression $\frac{3}{4}n_{x}^{6}$ is a scalar value, not a set of indices. For an $n \times n$ matrix, indices must range over a set like $\{1,2,...,n\}$ or $[n]$. The current notation makes the definition of $\mathcal{L}_t$ ill-formed. Rewrite $\mathcal{L}_{t}:=(\ell(a_{ti}|z_{tji}))_{i,j\in\frac{3}{4}n_{x}^{6}}$ as $\mathcal{L}_{t}:=(\ell(a_{ti}|z_{tji}))_{i,j\in\{1,2,...,n\}}$.

comments.title: Inconsistent interpretation of IC3Net performance
comments.quote: We find that IC3Net, with its information-hiding gate, performs less well than CommNet but performs better when trained in TSP due to the truthful mechanism.
comments.explanation: This statement contradicts the data in Table 2. IC3Net performs better than CommNet in both tasks (2.276 > 1.592 for Explore, 3.815 > 3.202 for Combat), not "less well". Furthermore, IC3Net with TSP performs worse than both IC3Net and CommNet, not "better". The text should be corrected to accurately state that IC3Net outperforms CommNet, and TSP reduces IC3Net's performance.

comments.title: Undefined notation in convergence condition
comments.quote: $\sup_{\phi}\left|\frac{\partial\operatorname{Re}V^{\pi_{\theta}}}{\partial\operatorname{Im}V^{\pi_{\theta}}}\right|<\beta,$
comments.explanation: The convergence condition uses undefined notation. The variable $\phi$ appears in the supremum but is not defined anywhere in the paper. Additionally, $V^{\pi_{\theta}}$ is not defined - it appears to be a value function, but its precise definition and relationship to the policy parameters $\theta$ is unclear. The partial derivatives with respect to real and imaginary parts also need clarification. Define all variables and notation used in Equation (9).

comments.title: Incomplete Hermitianity argument
comments.quote: Since the score matrix $\mathcal{L}_{t}$ does not satisfy Hermitianity, we perform zero-averaging by subtracting the mean of the scores from each element of the matrix, thereby making the sum to zero.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that zero-averaging makes the matrix Hermitian, but this is incorrect. Zero-averaging (making rows sum to zero) does not guarantee Hermitianity, which requires the matrix to equal its conjugate transpose. For a real matrix, Hermitianity reduces to symmetry: $A = A^T$. The paper needs to show that $\Delta\mathcal{L}_t$ is skew-symmetric ($\Delta\mathcal{L}_t = -(\Delta\mathcal{L}_t)^T$) to ensure that $i\Delta\mathcal{L}_t$ is Hermitian. The current argument conflates zero-sum property with Hermitianity.

comments.title: Logical gap in truthfulness to optimality claim
comments.quote: since achieving truthfulness is essentially the same problem as minimizing $D_{\phi}$, eventually it also maximizes $J^{*}$ will be maximized.
comments.explanation: The claim that minimizing $D_{\phi}$ maximizes $J^{*}$ omits the symmetric reward condition required by Proposition 3.1. The proposition explicitly states that both $D_{\phi}(\mathbf{Z}_{t})=\mathbb{H}[p(s_{t})]$ AND symmetric rewards $\mathcal{R}_{i}$ are needed for $J^{*}=\hat{J}$. Without symmetric rewards, truthfulness alone does not guarantee optimality. Rewrite the sentence as "since achieving truthfulness minimizes $D_{\phi}$, and under symmetric reward conditions this maximizes $J^{*}$, eventually $J^{*}$ will be maximized" to include the necessary condition.

comments.title: Undefined observation probability notation
comments.quote: $\mathcal{P}\in\mathfrak{P}\left(\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{X}^{n}\right)$ is an observation probability, and
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathfrak{P}$ is indeed undefined and should be clarified. The domain $\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{X}^{n}$ suggests $\mathcal{P}$ is a joint distribution over states and all agents' observations. However, the paper later references individual observations $x_{ti}$. This creates ambiguity about whether $\mathcal{P}(s_t, x_{t1}, ..., x_{tn})$ is the fundamental object or if individual observation probabilities $\mathcal{P}_i(s_t, x_{ti})$ are needed. Add: "where $\mathfrak{P}(\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{X}^{n})$ denotes the set of probability distributions over $\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{X}^{n}$, and $\mathcal{P}(s_t, x_{t1}, ..., x_{tn})$ represents the joint probability of state $s_t$ and all agents' observations" after the observation probability definition.

comments.title: Spelling errors in technical terms
comments.quote: We formulate the non-cooperative partially observable environment as an extention of the *pertially observable stochastic games* (POSG) *(Hansen et al., 2004)*;
comments.explanation: There are two spelling errors in this sentence. "extention" should be "extension" and "pertially" should be "partially". These are clear typographical errors that affect the readability of the technical description. Rewrite as: "We formulate the non-cooperative partially observable environment as an extension of the *partially observable stochastic games* (POSG) *(Hansen et al., 2004)*;"

comments.title: Undefined notation in message vector
comments.quote: $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{ti}:=\mathbf{Z}_{t:i}^{\mathsf{T}}=(z_{t1i},\ldots,z_{t,i-1,i},h_{ti},z_{t,i+1,i},\ldots,z_{tni})^{\mathsf{T}}$
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathbf{Z}_{t:i}^{\mathsf{T}}$ appears without definition. While $\mathbf{Z}_{t:ij} = z_{tij}$ was defined, the colon notation $\mathbf{Z}_{t:i}$ is not standard and creates ambiguity about whether it refers to column $i$, row $i$, or another matrix slice. Add an explicit definition: 'Let $\mathbf{Z}_{t:i}$ denote the $i$-th column of matrix $\mathbf{Z}_t$' or replace with standard notation $\mathbf{Z}_t[:,i]$ for clarity.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces 'Truthful Self-Play' (TSP), a framework that integrates peer prediction mechanisms with multi-agent reinforcement learning to incentivize honest communication in partially observable, non-cooperative environments. By augmenting rewards with imaginary components derived from peer prediction, the authors claim to guide agents toward global optima in communicative stochastic games.

The paper tackles a significant challenge in multi-agent learning: the emergence of deceptive signaling in competitive settings. The proposed use of mechanism design to structure the reward function is an elegant theoretical step. However, the connection between the theoretical convergence proofs and the practical implementation in deep neural networks remains underspecified.

Overview issues:

- Unclear convergence conditions in Theorem 4.1: Theorem 4.1 provides a convergence guarantee to the global optimum, but the condition in Equation 9, involving the ratio of gradients of the real and imaginary value functions, is not sufficiently grounded in the dynamics of the learning process. It is unclear how one would verify or enforce this bound in practice, especially given that the imaginary reward is non-stationary and depends on the evolving policy. The paper should explain whether this condition is a requirement for the existence of an equilibrium or a condition on the learning rate and policy updates. Without a clearer mapping from the neural network parameters to this bound, the theoretical claim of global optimality lacks practical bite.

- Collusion vulnerability in decentralized peer prediction: The peer prediction mechanism in Section 4.2 assumes that the score matrix $\mathcal{L}_t$ effectively penalizes non-truthful agents. However, in non-cooperative settings, agents can collude to report signals that maximize their collective imaginary reward at the expense of truthfulness. The paper does not address whether the proposed zero-averaging (Equation 7) or the specific scoring rule (Equation 6) is robust to such coordinated deviations. A brief analysis or a counter-example showing how the mechanism handles a coalition of agents would significantly strengthen the claim of robustness.

- Information leakage and state representation: The paper claims that TSP leads to unbiased state representations, but the communication protocol itself relies on latent representations within RNNs. There is no analysis of whether the peer prediction mechanism inadvertently leaks information about an agent's private state $h_{ti}$ to an adversary. If an adversary can infer the private state from the messages or the imaginary reward feedback, the incentive compatibility of the truthful equilibrium may collapse. The authors should clarify if the 'truthfulness' achieved is robust to agents that are actively trying to infer and exploit the private states of their peers.

- Lack of ablation on the imaginary reward structure: The framework relies on the specific construction of the imaginary reward $\mathbf{R}_t^+ = \mathbf{R}_t + i\Delta \mathcal{L}_t$. While the authors show that this construction sums to zero, it is not clear why this specific Laplacian-based transformation is the optimal way to force truthfulness. The paper would benefit from an ablation study comparing this construction against other zero-sum reward modifications or alternative scoring rules. Without such a comparison, it is difficult to determine if the performance gains are due to the peer prediction mechanism itself or merely the introduction of an auxiliary, regularizing signal.

- Absence of a formal counterexample for non-truthful equilibria: The paper claims that TSP converges to the global optimum, yet it does not demonstrate a specific case where standard self-play fails to converge due to a lack of truthfulness while TSP succeeds. To provide bite to this claim, the authors should compute the equilibrium for a simple, fully-specified 2-agent Comm-POSG (such as a 2-state, 2-action coordination game with asymmetric information) and show analytically that the standard self-play BNE is suboptimal, whereas the TSP equilibrium recovers the global optimum. This would bridge the gap between the general theoretical framework and the specific multi-agent environments tested.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for the mass parameter beta: The theoretical convergence result in Theorem 4.1 depends on the mass parameter beta, which regulates the influence of imaginary rewards. However, the paper provides no guidance on how to choose this parameter or how sensitive the convergence is to its value. A simulation study showing the performance of TSP across a range of beta values in one of the provided environments (e.g., Predator-Prey) would be necessary to demonstrate that the framework is robust and not overly dependent on specific hyperparameter tuning.

- Missing comparison to non-communicative benchmarks: While the paper compares TSP against other communicative baselines like IC3Net, it does not explicitly compare the 'truthfulness' of the learned representation against a baseline that does not use any communication protocol. To justify the overhead of the peer prediction mechanism, the authors should show that the performance gain is not simply due to increased signal volume, but specifically due to the truthful nature of the signals. A comparison against a version of the environment where communication is forced to be truthful (e.g., via an oracle) would provide a clear upper bound on the benefits of the TSP mechanism.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Clarify the practical verification or enforcement of the convergence condition in Equation 9 during training.

- Add a discussion or a small-scale experiment addressing the robustness of the peer prediction mechanism against colluding agents.

- Include an ablation study comparing the proposed imaginary reward construction with alternative zero-sum regularizers.

- Expand the theoretical section to explicitly address the potential for information leakage through the communication channel.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of global convergence
comments.quote: 1. Strong convergence: TSP guarantees convergence to the global optimum theoretically and experimentally; self-play cannot provide such a guarantee.
comments.explanation: The claim that TSP provides a theoretical guarantee of convergence to the global optimum is stated without the necessary qualifications derived in Section 4.3. Theorem 4.1 establishes convergence only under specific constraints on the gradient ratio, which are not universally satisfied in general MARL environments. By omitting these conditions in the summary, the text implies a stronger result than the proof supports. Please qualify this statement to explicitly mention that convergence is guaranteed only under the conditions specified in Theorem 4.1.

comments.title: Inconsistency in social welfare definition
comments.quote: $J:=\sum_{i=1}^{n}V^{\pi_{i}};\qquad V^{\pi_{i}}:=\mathbb{E}_{\pi_{i}}\left[\sum_{t=1}^{T}\gamma^{t-1}r_{ti}\right],$ (1)
comments.explanation: The definition of social welfare in Equation (1) is problematic because it suggests that each agent's value function is computed under its own policy in isolation. In a POSG, the reward is a function of the joint action profile. Consequently, the expectation must be taken over the joint policy. The current notation implies that the expectation is only over the marginal policy of agent i, which fails to account for the strategic coupling inherent in the reward function. Please rewrite Equation (1) to reflect the dependence on the joint policy profile.

comments.title: Missing condition for Hermitian property
comments.quote: Proposition 4.1. For any  $\mathcal{G}$  in Comm-POSG, if  $\mathcal{G}[i\mathbf{Y}]$  is truthful and  $\mathbf{R}^{+}$  is an Hermitian matrix,  $J^{*}(\mathcal{G}[i\mathbf{Y}]) = \hat{J} (\mathcal{G})$  holds.
comments.explanation: The proof of Proposition 4.1 relies on the assumption that R+ is Hermitian, which implies iy_ij = -iy_ji. However, the score matrix L_t defined in Equation (3) is not generally symmetric, as the log-likelihood of an action given a signal is not necessarily symmetric across agents. Consequently, the Laplacian is not guaranteed to be skew-symmetric, and R+ is not guaranteed to be Hermitian. Please clarify how the construction of L_t ensures the Hermitian property of R+ or include a projection step to enforce it.

comments.title: Unjustified leap from objective equality to convergence
comments.quote: Therefore, from Proposition 4.1, convergence to the global optima is achieved. ∎
comments.explanation: The proof relies on Proposition 4.1 to bridge the gap between the existence of a truthful mechanism and the convergence of the learning dynamics. However, Proposition 4.1 only establishes an equality of objectives under the assumption of truthfulness; it does not address the stability or convergence of the policy gradient updates used in the TSP algorithm. The convergence of the learning process requires showing that the stochastic approximation updates actually reach the global optimum. Please include an argument demonstrating that the learning dynamics are stable and converge to the global optimum under the condition in Equation 9.

comments.title: Inconsistency between reported scores and optimality claim
comments.quote: We can see that IC3Net with TSP outperforms the one with SP for all tasks.
comments.explanation: The text claims that TSP outperforms SP for all tasks, but the data in Table 1 shows that for PP-5, the IC3Net (SP) score is 2.44 ± 0.18, while the IC3Net (TSP) score is 2.34 ± 0.21. Given that the standard deviations overlap significantly, the claim of consistent outperformance is not supported by the provided data for this task. Please revise the claim to reflect the statistical insignificance of the difference in the PP-5 task.

comments.title: Ambiguity in signal matrix definition
comments.quote: The $i$-th diagonal entry of $\mathbf{Z}_{t}$, $h_{ti}:=z_{tii}$ represents the *pre-state*, an internal state of $i$-th agent before receiving the singals from the others.
comments.explanation: The definition of the signal matrix includes the diagonal entries as 'pre-states', yet the matrix is defined as the 'complete information exchanged among the agent'. If $z_{tii}$ is an internal state and not an exchanged signal, it is unclear why it is included in the matrix of exchanged signals. Furthermore, if $z_{tii}$ is an internal state, it should be part of the agent's state space rather than the signal space. Please clarify if $z_{tii}$ is a signal sent from an agent to itself or if it is a distinct variable that should be excluded from the communication matrix.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Truthful Self-Play (TSP), a framework that adds imaginary rewards derived from a peer prediction mechanism to encourage honest state sharing in non-cooperative partially observable stochastic games. While the motivation to address information asymmetry in self-play is relevant, the theoretical foundation contains significant gaps, the experimental claims contradict the reported numbers, and the mechanism misapplies established results from mechanism design. The work requires substantial theoretical and empirical revisions before it can be considered for publication.

The paper tackles an important problem in multi-agent reinforcement learning: preventing information hiding in non-cooperative partially observable settings. The idea of borrowing mechanism design to regularize communication is creative, and the experimental setup covers standard benchmarks. However, the theoretical claims overreach the actual derivation, the peer prediction adaptation lacks formal justification, and the empirical results contain confusing sign conventions and missing baselines that weaken the overall contribution.

Overview issues:

- The convergence proof relies on unverified assumptions and omits learning dynamics: Theorem 4.1 asserts that TSP converges to the global optimum under the condition in Equation 9, but the provided proof consists of a single sentence that merely restates Proposition 4.1. It completely ignores the non-stationarity inherent in self-play and the approximation errors introduced by neural networks. The condition involving the partial derivative of the real and imaginary value functions is never defined in terms of observable quantities, nor is there any analysis showing how the policy gradient actually drives the system toward this condition. Without demonstrating that the imaginary rewards create a valid Lyapunov function or that the peer prediction scores remain incentive-compatible under distribution shift, the convergence claim remains unsubstantiated. The authors should replace the hand-wavy proof with a formal analysis of the learning dynamics under the proposed reward structure, or explicitly state that the theorem only holds for tabular, stationary settings.

- The peer prediction mechanism is misapplied and incompatible with online policy updates: Section 4.2 claims to adapt the peer prediction method by setting the score to $\log\pi_{\theta}(a_{ti}|z_{tji})$. Standard peer prediction relies on proper scoring rules that evaluate reported beliefs against a fixed prior, but here the prior $\pi_{\theta}$ is continuously updated during training. This violates the core assumption that agents share a stable belief distribution. Furthermore, peer prediction is designed to elicit private information by comparing reports, not to evaluate actions. Using the action log-likelihood as a scoring rule does not guarantee truthfulness and effectively reduces to a standard policy gradient baseline. The mechanism needs to be rederived to account for the fact that agents are optimizing a changing policy, or the authors should adopt a verification-free method that does not require a static prior.

- Experimental results contradict the stated performance claims and reward conventions: The text states that IC3Net with TSP outperforms the baseline with self-play for all tasks, yet Table 1 and Table 2 report numerically lower values for TSP compared to baselines. The table caption notes that all scores are negatives, but the actual values listed are positive, creating a direct contradiction between the notation and the data. If higher social welfare is better, TSP performs worse in several cases. If lower is better, the sign convention is confusing and poorly explained. The authors must clarify the reward sign convention, reconcile the table values with the text, and ensure that the bolded values actually correspond to the claimed improvements.

- The proposed algorithm never implements the theoretical truthfulness metric: Section 3.3 introduces $D_{\phi}$ as a measure of information asymmetry and claims that minimizing it maximizes social welfare. Proposition 3.1 then links $D_{\phi}=\mathbb{H}[p(s_t)]$ to global optimality. However, Algorithm 1 never computes or minimizes $D_{\phi}$. Instead, the update rule relies entirely on the imaginary reward $i\Delta\mathcal{L}_t$ derived from action log-likelihoods. The paper provides no mathematical bridge explaining how the imaginary reward approximates or bounds $D_{\phi}$. Without this connection, the theoretical justification for the mechanism is disconnected from the actual implementation. The authors should either modify the algorithm to explicitly regularize $D_{\phi}$ or derive a formal equivalence between the imaginary reward and the information asymmetry metric.

- The scoring mechanism requires centralized policy knowledge that contradicts the decentralized setting: Computing the peer prediction score $\ell_{tij} = \log\pi_{\theta}(a_{ti}|z_{tji})$ requires Agent $i$ to know the policy parameters of Agent $j$ or to have access to the joint policy. Section 3.2 explicitly states that all agents share the same weights per episode, which means the method operates under a parameter-sharing or centralized training paradigm rather than a fully decentralized one. The paper does not address how agents obtain $\pi_{\theta}$ during training, nor does it discuss the communication overhead of sharing policy weights. If parameter sharing is assumed, the method collapses into a centralized training with decentralized execution setup, which undermines the claimed novelty of a fully decentralized truthful mechanism. The implementation details must clarify the information flow, and the theoretical claims should be adjusted to match the actual information constraints.

- Insufficient baselines and ablation studies weaken the empirical validation: The experimental section only compares TSP against vanilla self-play and self-play with curiosity. It omits modern communication-aware MARL baselines such as QMIX, MAPPO, or dedicated communication methods like A3C-Comm and DIAL. Without these comparisons, it is impossible to determine whether the performance gains stem from the proposed mechanism or simply from the use of IC3Net gating architecture. Additionally, the paper lacks ablation studies isolating the contribution of the imaginary reward, the peer prediction score, and the mass parameter $\beta$. A controlled experiment varying $\beta$ and removing the imaginary component would clarify whether the complex reward structure provides any actual benefit over standard regularization. The authors should expand the baseline comparison and include ablations to substantiate the empirical claims.

- Concrete parametric example for the peer prediction mechanism: The theoretical framework defines Comm-POSG and the imaginary reward in highly abstract terms, leaving readers without a concrete reference for how the mechanism operates. Computing the peer prediction score and imaginary reward for a simple, fully-specified model—such as a two-agent coordination game or a linear-quadratic stochastic game—would let readers calibrate their intuitions. In such a model, the authors could explicitly show how the score matrix is constructed, how the Laplacian averaging enforces the zero-sum property, and how the resulting gradient update modifies the policy trajectory. Without this worked example, it is difficult to verify whether the proposed mechanism actually satisfies the claimed properties in a tractable setting. Readers will also benefit from seeing how the mass parameter scales the update in this concrete case.

- Example demonstrating escape from local optima via imaginary rewards: The paper claims that the imaginary reward modifies the gradient of local optima without altering the global optima, yet provides no demonstration that this distinction actually holds in practice. A natural test would be to construct a simple multi-agent environment with a known bad local equilibrium, such as a prisoner's dilemma variant or a communication bottleneck task, and show that standard self-play gets stuck there. The authors should then run TSP on the same environment and plot the policy trajectories or value functions to verify that the imaginary reward successfully pushes the agents toward the global optimum.
